---
title: "Ch 14 — Probability (Axiomatic Approach)"
subtitle: "From sample spaces and events to Kolmogorov's three axioms"
lesson-id: "cbse11.probability"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 14
ncert-chapter-title: "Probability"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse10.probability", "cbse11.sets"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.probability"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "encryption", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state the Class 10 definition $P(E) = \dfrac{\text{favourable}}{\text{total}}$?
2. Are you fluent with set operations $\cup, \cap, '$ from
   **[Sets](../01-sets/index.qmd)**?
3. Can you list the sample space when two coins are tossed?
4. Are you comfortable with the word "event" meaning "a subset of the
   sample space"?

Three confident yeses — read on.
:::

## Touch

Toss two coins. The possible outcomes are:

$$ S = \{HH,\ HT,\ TH,\ TT\}. $$

This is the **sample space**. Now consider:

- $A$ = "first coin is heads" $= \{HH, HT\}$.
- $B$ = "exactly one head" $= \{HT, TH\}$.

Each is a *subset* of $S$ — that's an **event**. The probability of an
event is the fraction of outcomes it contains, **assuming each outcome
is equally likely**.

Use the widget to enumerate sample spaces for various dice and coin
combinations.

```{ojs}
viewof exper = Inputs.select(
  ["coin", "two coins", "die", "two dice (sum)", "card draw"],
  {label: "Experiment"}
)
```

```{ojs}
{
  let s, n;
  if (exper === "coin") { s = "H, T"; n = 2; }
  else if (exper === "two coins") { s = "HH, HT, TH, TT"; n = 4; }
  else if (exper === "die") { s = "1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6"; n = 6; }
  else if (exper === "two dice (sum)") {
    s = "2, 3, ..., 12 (with multiplicity); 36 ordered pairs"; n = 36;
  }
  else { s = "52 cards (13 ranks × 4 suits)"; n = 52; }
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Experiment:</b> ${exper}</div>
    <div><b>Sample space:</b> ${s}</div>
    <div><b>|S| = ${n}</b> equally likely outcomes (assuming fairness).</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Probability is just counting — but counting *very carefully*.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. Toss two coins. Why is "exactly one head" *not* probability $1/3$,
   even though there are three "kinds" of outcome (0H, 1H, 2H)?
2. The probability of an event $A$ is between $0$ and $1$. What event
   has probability $0$? Probability $1$?
3. If $P(A) = 0.4$ and $P(B) = 0.5$ and $P(A \cap B) = 0.1$, what is
   $P(A \cup B)$? Is it $0.9$? Or something else?
4. The event "either $A$ or $B$" — is its probability the sum
   $P(A) + P(B)$? When?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) is the eternal trap: outcomes must be *equally likely*. The four
ordered pairs are equally likely; the three "kinds" are not. (4) is the
**addition rule**: works as a sum *only* when $A$ and $B$ are disjoint.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Sample space

A die is rolled twice. Write the sample space.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$36$ ordered pairs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$S = \{(i, j) : 1 \le i, j \le 6\}$, $|S| = 36$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Probability of an event

Find the probability that the sum is $7$ when a die is rolled twice.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pairs summing to $7$: $(1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6$ favourable out of $36$. $P = 6/36 = 1/6$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Complement

If $P(A) = 3/8$, find $P(A')$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(A') = 1 - P(A) = 5/8$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Addition rule

If $P(A) = 0.4$, $P(B) = 0.5$, $P(A \cap B) = 0.1$, find $P(A \cup B)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(A \cup B) = 0.4 + 0.5 - 0.1 = 0.8$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Mutually exclusive

A bag has 5 red, 3 blue, 4 green balls. One ball is drawn. Find the
probability it is red **or** blue.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Red" and "blue" are mutually exclusive: $P(R \cup B) = P(R) + P(B)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(R) = 5/12$, $P(B) = 3/12$. $P(R \cup B) = 8/12 = 2/3$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Card problem

A card is drawn from a standard $52$-card deck. Find the probability
the card is a heart **or** a king.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$13$ hearts, $4$ kings, $1$ king of hearts (in the overlap).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(H) + P(K) - P(H \cap K) = 13/52 + 4/52 - 1/52 = 16/52 = 4/13$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Three coins

Three coins are tossed. Find the probability of getting at least two
heads.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|S| = 8$. Outcomes with $\ge 2$ heads: pick which $2$ are H, plus all
$3$ H.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At least two heads: $\binom{3}{2} + \binom{3}{3} = 3 + 1 = 4$ outcomes
out of $8$. $P = 4/8 = 1/2$.
</details>

You have just used [**sample spaces**](/glossary.qmd#sample-space),
**events as subsets**, the
[**probability function**](/glossary.qmd#probability), the **complement
rule**, the **addition rule**, **mutually exclusive events**, and
**counting via [combinations](/glossary.qmd#combination)**. Now we name
them.

::: {.fun-fact}
**Kolmogorov axiomatises probability (1933).** Pascal and Fermat
exchanged the founding letters in 1654; Bernoulli, Laplace, and Bayes
built the calculation rules over the next two centuries. But until 1933
nobody had answered "what *is* a probability, formally?" with anything
better than counting equally-likely outcomes — a definition that breaks
the moment outcomes are not finite or not equally likely. In a slim
booklet titled *Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung*, the
Soviet mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov gave probability the same
treatment Euclid gave geometry: three axioms (non-negativity,
normalisation, countable additivity) and let everything else fall out
as theorems. The three axioms in *Name* are his — distilled to fit a
Class 11 page.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Probability is "fraction of outcomes" — but only when outcomes are
equally likely.** Get the sample space right and the rest is
arithmetic. The addition rule
$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ is the universal version;
disjoint events let you drop the overlap. Three reflexes carry the
chapter: list the sample space, ask whether outcomes are equally likely,
and use the complement when "at least one" appears.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Sample space** $S$: the set of all outcomes of the experiment.

**Event** $E$: a subset $E \subseteq S$.

**Equally-likely outcomes:** if all $|S|$ outcomes are equally likely,
then for any event $E$,
$$ P(E) = \dfrac{|E|}{|S|}. $$

**Kolmogorov's axioms** (for any sample space):

1. $P(E) \ge 0$ for every event $E$.
2. $P(S) = 1$.
3. If $E_1, E_2$ are mutually exclusive ($E_1 \cap E_2 = \varnothing$):
   $P(E_1 \cup E_2) = P(E_1) + P(E_2)$.

**Consequences:**

- $P(\varnothing) = 0$.
- $P(E') = 1 - P(E)$.
- $0 \le P(E) \le 1$.
- **Addition rule (general):** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.

**Three-event addition:**
$$P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) + P(A \cap B \cap C).$$

:::

A short verse:

> *Outcomes equally likely — count and divide;*
> *Add when disjoint, subtract overlap aside.*
> *Complement adds to one;*
> *And every probability lies between zero and one.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A bag contains $4$ red and $6$ black balls. Two are drawn at random
> *without replacement*. Find the probability that both are red.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Total ways to draw $2$ balls: $\binom{10}{2} = 45$.
2. Ways to draw $2$ red: $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.
3. $P = 6/45 = 2/15$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A die is rolled. Probability of an even number?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3/6 = 1/2$.
</details>

**W2.** Two coins tossed. Probability of two tails?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1/4$.
</details>

**W3.** A card drawn. Probability of a face card (J, Q, K)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$12/52 = 3/13$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A bag contains $5$ red and $3$ blue balls. Two are drawn at
random. Find the probability that they are of different colours.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total ways: $\binom{8}{2} = 28$. Different colours: pick 1 red and 1 blue.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Different colours: $5 \times 3 = 15$. $P = 15/28$.
</details>

**P2.** Three letters are randomly placed in three envelopes (each
envelope has its own correct letter). What is the probability that
none of the letters is in its correct envelope?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The number of permutations with no fixed point is the **derangement**
number. For 3 letters, this equals 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total permutations: $3! = 6$. Derangements: $2$ (the cycles
$1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$ and $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1$).

$P = 2/6 = 1/3$.
</details>

**P3.** From a group of $7$ men and $5$ women, a committee of $4$ is
chosen. Find the probability that the committee has exactly $2$ women.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total ways: $\binom{12}{4} = 495$. Favourable: $\binom{5}{2}\binom{7}{2} = 10 \times 21 = 210$.

$P = 210/495 = 14/33$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Two dice are rolled. Find the probability that the sum is a
prime number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Possible sums: $2$ to $12$. Primes among them: $2, 3, 5, 7, 11$.
Count outcomes for each.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum $2$: $1$ way. Sum $3$: $2$. Sum $5$: $4$. Sum $7$: $6$. Sum $11$: $2$.

Total: $1 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 2 = 15$.

$P = 15/36 = 5/12$.
</details>

**S2.** A class has $25$ students. Find the probability that at least
two have the same birthday (assume $365$ equally likely days, no leap
years). State the answer to two decimals.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the complement: $P(\text{all different}) = \dfrac{365}{365} \cdot \dfrac{364}{365} \cdots \dfrac{341}{365}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(\text{all different}) = \prod_{k=0}^{24} \dfrac{365 - k}{365} \approx 0.4313$.

$P(\text{some match}) \approx 1 - 0.4313 = 0.5687 \approx 0.57$.

This is the famous **birthday paradox** — over $50$% chance with just
$23$ people.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** If $P(A) = 0.4$ and $P(B) = 0.3$ and $A, B$ are
mutually exclusive, then $P(A \cup B)$ equals
(a) $0.12$ (b) $0.58$ (c) $0.7$ (d) $1.0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Mutually exclusive $\Rightarrow A \cap B = \varnothing \Rightarrow
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) = 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7$. **Answer: (c).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Two unbiased dice are rolled. Find the
probability of getting a doublet (same number on both).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Doublets: $(1,1), (2,2), \ldots, (6,6)$ — $6$ outcomes. $P = 6/36 = 1/6$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** From a deck of $52$ cards, a card is drawn
at random. Find the probability that it is (i) an ace, (ii) a face card,
(iii) a red card or a king.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $4/52 = 1/13$.

**(ii)** $12/52 = 3/13$.

**(iii)** Red cards: $26$. Kings: $4$. Red kings (in overlap): $2$.
$P = (26 + 4 - 2)/52 = 28/52 = 7/13$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** A box contains $4$ defective and $6$
non-defective items. Three items are drawn at random *without
replacement*.

(i) Find the probability that all three are non-defective.
(ii) Find the probability that exactly two are defective.
(iii) Find the probability that at least one is defective.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total: $\binom{10}{3} = 120$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\binom{6}{3}/\binom{10}{3} = 20/120 = 1/6$.

**(ii)** $\binom{4}{2}\binom{6}{1}/\binom{10}{3} = 6 \times 6/120 = 36/120 = 3/10$.

**(iii)** Complement of "all non-defective": $1 - 1/6 = 5/6$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 14 LA "card-drawing combined
events" matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the birthday paradox (Stretch S2) is JEE Advanced
adjacent — practice the complement-then-product technique.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"All outcomes are equally likely."** Sample-space outcomes must be
   defined so they *are* equally likely. "Three kinds of outcome"
   (0 heads, 1 head, 2 heads) is *not* equally likely; the four ordered
   pairs $HH, HT, TH, TT$ are.
2. **"$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ always."** Only when $A \cap B =
   \varnothing$. In general, subtract the overlap.
3. **"Probabilities can be greater than $1$."** They cannot. If your
   formula gives $> 1$, either the events aren't disjoint (and you
   need to subtract) or the question is malformed.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Sample space first, then count.** Make sure the outcomes you list
   are *equally likely* — that single check rules out most beginner
   errors.
2. **Addition rule is "add, then remove the double-count."** $P(A \cup
   B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Disjoint events make the overlap
   zero.
3. **"At least one" begs for the complement.** $P(\text{at least one}) =
   1 - P(\text{none})$ usually beats summing many disjoint cases.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you list the sample space for "draw $2$ cards from a deck"
   and count the outcomes for "both red"?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you decide when to use the addition rule vs the complement vs
   the multiplication of independent events?

- **3 yes:** you have completed Class 11 — congratulations!
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** drill the addition rule on five fresh two-event problems.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Class 10 — Probability](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/14-probability/index.qmd)** —
  the favourable/total ratio for equally likely outcomes.
- **[Sets](../01-sets/index.qmd)** — events are subsets; operations
  $\cup, \cap, '$ are the heart of probability algebra.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Probability](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/13a-conditional-probability-bayes/index.qmd)** —
  conditional probability, Bayes' theorem, random variables, binomial
  and normal distributions.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 14.1–14.4: random experiments, events, axiomatic
  probability, addition rule.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t3.basic-probability`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/basic-probability/index.qmd) and
  [`t3.conditional-probability`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/conditional-probability/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every machine learning model encodes probabilities —
class-conditional likelihoods, posterior beliefs, attention weights.
Classifiers output $P(\text{class} \mid \text{features})$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** A cipher is "secure" if the probability of guessing
the plaintext from the ciphertext is essentially $1/|\text{key space}|$.
Cryptanalysis is probability theory weaponised.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Statistical hypothesis testing rejects null hypotheses
when the observed event has $P < 0.05$. The whole machinery of
$p$-values starts from the axioms you've just learned.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: NRICH (the birthday paradox), Russian school (Discover
problems force the addition rule before it is named), Bhāskara (verse
on disjoint vs overlapping events), Better Explained ("counting very
carefully" framing).
:::
